summary:
The domestication of the pig (Sus scrofa domesticus) from the Eurasian wild boar occurred independently in at least two distinct geographic centers: Anatolia in the Near East and the Yangtze and Mekong River regions of China, both processes beginning around 7000 to 6000 BCE. Genetic studies have confirmed that domestic pigs in different parts of the world trace their ancestry back to these separate domestication events rather than to a single origin, demonstrating that humans in different parts of the world arrived at the same solution to the challenge of securing a reliable pork supply.

In Anatolia, pig domestication appears to have occurred in close association with the broader Neolithic farming complex, with pigs found at sites such as [[event:11|Çatalhöyük]] and other early farming villages. In China, pigs were independently domesticated from the Chinese wild boar subspecies, becoming a central element of the agricultural system that supported growing Neolithic populations in the Yellow and Yangtze river valleys. Archaeological sites associated with cultures such as the [[event:1424|Hemudu culture]] provide early evidence of pig domestication in East Asia.

Pigs presented unique advantages as domestic animals: they are highly efficient converters of organic food waste into protein, reproduce rapidly, require no grazing land, and can subsist on scraps and foraged material. These characteristics made them ideal additions to early farming villages, where food waste was abundant. The pig's subsequent spread across Eurasia and its eventual global distribution reflect these practical advantages, though cultural and religious prohibitions in some societies have also shaped the geographical distribution of pig husbandry throughout history.
